senses.definition: a choice made in a situation in which no other options were available
‌to choose the most desirable alternative out of the ones available

examples.x: The fashionista enjoyed shopping at the boutique because she could pick and choose from a variety of stylish outfits.
The job applicant was in a fortunate position where multiple companies were interested in hiring him, allowing him to pick and choose the best offer.
As a book lover, she relished the opportunity to visit the library and pick and choose from a vast collection of novels.
The restaurant had a buffet where diners could pick and choose from a wide range of dishes to create their own customized meals.
The curator had the privilege of examining a collection of rare artifacts and could pick and choose which pieces to include in the museum exhibition.

senses.definition: a choice or option that seems less harmful or unpleasant out of two that one is confronted with

examples.x: In the election, voters felt they had to choose the lesser of two evils, as both candidates had significant flaws.
The company had to decide between laying off employees or reducing salaries, opting for the lesser of two evils to minimize the impact on the workforce.
In the film, the protagonist had to decide whether to betray a friend or face dire consequences—a classic example of choosing the lesser of two evils.

senses.definition: a situation in which there is too much of resources or options for one to choose from

examples.x: The art collector had an embarrassment of riches with a vast collection of masterpieces that even museums would envy.
The talented musician had an embarrassment of riches when it came to career opportunities, making it difficult to choose which path to pursue.
The popular author faced an embarrassment of riches with numerous book deals and publishing offers flooding in after the success of their debut novel.
The restaurant's menu offered an embarrassment of riches, leaving diners overwhelmed by the wide variety of delicious options to choose from.
The entrepreneur found themselves in an embarrassment of riches with multiple investors willing to fund their startup, making it challenging to decide which offer to accept.

senses.definition: used for saying that choosing out of the two possible options is not really going to make a difference as the result will be the same either way

examples.x: When it comes to choosing between the two apartments, it's as broad as it is long – they both have similar rent, location, and amenities.
The debate over which smartphone brand to buy is broad as it is long, as both options offer similar features and performance.
Whether we take the scenic route or the highway, it's as broad as it is long – both routes have similar travel times due to current road conditions.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪn mˈaɪnd

senses.definition: to consider someone or something when doing or mentioning something

examples.x: When we were discussing candidates for the job opening, the manager mentioned that she had a few experienced individuals in mind.
The teacher asked the students to suggest a book for the class reading assignment, but she already had a classic novel in mind.
As we were planning the event, the event organizer mentioned that she had a specific venue in mind that would be perfect for our needs.
When my friends asked me for restaurant recommendations, I had a cozy Italian trattoria in mind that serves amazing pasta dishes.

senses.definition: to choose something out of two or a group of things available to one

examples.x: The dessert buffet was filled with delectable treats, so go ahead and have your pick.
With a wide range of colors available, you can take your pick and choose the perfect shade for your new car.
The bookstore had a vast selection of novels, allowing readers to take their pick and find their next favorite book.
The job applicant was thrilled to receive multiple job offers and had the opportunity to have their pick.
At the farmers market, you can take your pick of fresh produce, from vibrant fruits to crisp vegetables.

word_list.word: [come] down on one side of the fence or the other
word_list.pronunciation: kˈʌm dˌaʊn ˌɑːn wˈʌn sˈaɪd ʌvðə fˈɛns ɔːɹ ðɪ ˈʌðɚ

senses.definition: to decide between two possible alternatives or choices that one has

examples.x: In the heated debate over climate change, scientists and policymakers need to come down on one side of the fence or the other to drive effective action.
The committee members couldn't reach a consensus on the proposal, so the chairperson urged them to come down on one side of the fence or the other.
When it comes to choosing a political party, it's important to come down on one side of the fence or the other to actively participate in the democratic process.
The CEO addressed the company's employees, urging them to come down on one side of the fence or the other regarding the new business strategy.
In the contentious legal case, the judge insisted that the jury members carefully consider the evidence and come down on one side of the fence or the other to deliver a fair verdict.

senses.definition: to consider something when trying to make a judgment or decision

examples.x: When planning the budget, it is crucial to take unexpected expenses into account to ensure financial stability.

senses.definition: to spend time considering one's future action before making a decision

examples.x: After receiving feedback on the project, he took some time to chew his cud and carefully consider how to incorporate the suggestions.
The writer liked to chew her cud before starting a new story, allowing ideas to percolate and take shape in her mind.
The team members gathered around the whiteboard, chewing their cud, as they brainstormed innovative solutions to the complex problem.
Before making a career change, she spent weeks chewing her cud, weighing the pros and cons of different options.
The professor encouraged the students to chew their cud while reading challenging texts, fostering a deeper understanding of the material.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡˌoʊ dˌaʊn ðæt ɔːɹ ðə ɹˈoʊd

senses.definition: to choose to do something in a specified manner

examples.x: He regretted going down the road of dishonesty, realizing the negative consequences it had on his relationships and reputation.

senses.definition: in a state that one is capable of making logical decisions or behaving normally

examples.x: She couldn't believe he would invest his life savings in such a risky venture, questioning if he was in his right mind.
The jury determined that the defendant was not in his right mind at the time of the crime due to mental illness.
When he proposed quitting his stable job to pursue his dreams of becoming an artist, his parents questioned if he was in his right mind.
The therapist reassured him that his fears were unfounded and that he was in his right mind.

senses.definition: the action of adopting a different opinion or mindset

examples.x: After years of estrangement, she had a change of heart and decided to reach out to her long-lost sister.
The politician had a change of heart on the controversial issue, publicly reversing their previous stance.
He was skeptical about the charity's mission at first, but a moving testimonial led to a change of heart, prompting him to become a dedicated supporter.
Despite initially opposing the project, the community had a change of heart when they realized the positive impact it would have on local businesses.
The strict teacher had a change of heart after witnessing the student's determination and granted them an extension on the assignment.

senses.definition: used to state that one has adopted a different opinion

examples.x: I initially agreed to the plan, but on second thought, I think it might be too risky.
I almost bought the expensive gadget, but on second thought, I decided to save the money.
She agreed to the proposal, but on second thought, she realized it wasn’t practical.
On second thought, I don't think I should lend him the car; it's not a good idea.
I was going to order pizza, but on second thought, I’ll cook dinner instead.

senses.definition: to adopt a different policy in face of a difficult or unexpected problem

examples.x: The company decided not to change CEOs midway through their strategic initiative, not wanting to change horses in midstream and disrupt important projects in progress.

Some political analysts warned that introducing a new healthcare plan halfway through the legislative session would be changing horses in midstream and jeopardize the chances of passing any reform that year.
Just months before the big product launch, the marketing team was hesitant to switch agencies for fear of changing horses in midstream so close to roll-out.
During contract negotiations, the union refused the owner's demand to substitute mediators, stating it was ill-advised to change horses midstream while both sides were still at the table.

senses.definition: a point in a person's life or a situation where they must make a critical decision or choose between two different options or paths

examples.x: After graduating from college, he faced a fork in the road and had to decide whether to pursue further education or start his career.
Their relationship reached a fork in the road when they had to decide whether to stay together or go their separate ways.
The CEO had to navigate a fork in the road and choose between downsizing or seeking additional funding
After years of indecision, he finally reached a fork in the road and had to choose between staying in his hometown or moving to pursue new opportunities.

word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk ɐ lˈɑːŋ ɔːɹ hˈɑːɹd lˈʊk æt ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to think about or consider something very carefully, particularly with the intention of improving it in mind

examples.x: The board of directors has decided to take a hard look at our expenditures and cut back on unnecessary spending.
As the new COO, part of my job is to take a long hard look at our operations and processes to find ways to increase efficiency.
Now that we've had a couple disappointing quarters, it's time for management to take a long hard look at our pricing strategy and whether changes are needed.
The recent security breaches really underscore how urgently we need to take a hard look at our cybersecurity protocols and shore up any vulnerabilities.

senses.definition: to change one's opinion or decision regarding something

examples.x: George didn't want to come, but we persuaded him to change his mind.
He wasn't going to come, but at the last minute he changed his mind.
If you change your mind about coming tonight, just give me a call.

senses.definition: to excuse or overlook something without criticism or consequences

examples.x: Despite its flaws, I decided to give the movie a pass because I enjoyed the overall story.
Although the dish lacked seasoning, the chef decided to give it a pass since the presentation was exceptional.

word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk mˈæɾɚz ɔːɹ ɐfˈɛɹz ɔːɹ θˈɪŋz ˌɪntʊ wˈʌnz ˈoʊn hˈændz

senses.definition: to do something oneself as opposed to waiting for others

examples.x: Seeing the financial struggles of her family, she took matters into her own hands and started a small business to generate additional income.
Realizing the importance of education, he took matters into his own hands and created a study group to help his classmates prepare for exams.
As the manager of the department, he realized he needed to take affairs into his own hands to improve efficiency and productivity.
When the company's profits were declining, the CEO knew it was time to take things into her own hands and develop a new business strategy.
